text: Greetings from Tata Memorial Hospital. Today we will be giving a video demonstration of robotic pelvic lymph node dissection for locally advanced rectal cancer in a patient who has received total neoadjuvant therapy. We have nothing to disclose.

Our patient is a fifty nine year old lady who presented to us with complaints of altered bowel habits since one month. On examination there was an ulcer proliferative growth one centimeter from the anal verge. Colonoscopy showed a growth in the lower rectum two centimeters from the anal verge. Remaining colonoscopy was normal and biopsy of the growth was suggestive of well differentiated adenocarcinoma. The MRI showed a low rectal growth with multiple mesorectal and enlarged left internal iliac lymph node. She received total neoadjuvant therapy in the form of short course radiation followed by four cycles of chemotherapy. The response scan showed a decrease in the rectal growth with mild decrease in mesorectal and left internal iliac and operator nodes, suggestive of a partial response. She was then planned for abdominal perineal resection with left lateral pelvic lymph node

The sagittal section displays a low rectal growth with a partial response, while the axial section reveals persistent lateral lymph nodes. The robotic setup includes a fenestrated bipolar in the R-I arm, a camera port in the R-II arm, a monopolar scissor in the R-III arm, and a prograsp in the R-IV arm. The surgeon begins by medializing the ureter and defining the urethrohypogastric fascia.

The surgeon dissects along the obliterated umbilical ligament, pulling it medially to define the vesico-hypogastric fascia and the paravesical space. The assistant aids by pulling the ligament medially, while the surgeon continues dissection in the loose areolar tissue, revealing the obturator nerve.

The surgeon dissects nodal tissue over the external iliac vessels, proceeding from lateral to medial. Tissue is cleared over the femoral canal and dissected off the medial border of the external iliac vein, pulling it medially. The dissection continues proximally towards the bifurcation of the iliac vessels, clearing nodal tissue and identifying the proximal part of the obturator nerve.

The surgeon identifies and carefully dissects the proximal part of the obturator nerve using a cold swipe technique, clearing nodal tissue around it. The obturator vessels are identified, dissected, clipped, and divided using monopolar scissors. A vessel feeding the nodal tissue is also dissected, clipped, and divided. The surgeon continues to clear nodal tissue over the internal iliac artery and the obturator nerve in the distal segment.

The surgeon dissects and clears all nodal tissue from the obturator nerve, highlighting the difference in dissection depth required for rectal adenocarcinoma compared to gynecological malignancies, as the procedure continues towards the pelvic floor.

The surgeon clears tissue over the obliterated umbilical ligament, dissecting down along its lateral aspect. The dissection continues along the branches of the internal iliac artery, progressing towards the inferior vesicle artery.

The surgeon clears tissue along the lateral aspect of the bladder, proceeding towards the pelvic floor. The inferior vesicle artery is encountered, and nodal tissue over the artery is meticulously cleared. The dissection then moves laterally over the pudendal vessels, which are clipped and divided after clearing all surrounding tissue.

The surgeon clears all nodal tissue over the pelvic floor, completing the pelvic lymph node dissection. The specimen is delivered in total after the nodal tissue is reflected and dissected off. The final field of dissection is inspected, revealing intact urethra hypogastric fascia and preserved hypogastric nerve on the medial aspect. Dissection along the branches of the internal iliac vessels shows the internal iliac vein crossing anterior to the branches of the internal iliac artery, a noted anatomical variation. All nodal tissue along the inferior vesicle artery and pelvic floor is cleared, with critical structures identified and preserved.

We are depicting the boundaries and the critical structures that must be identified and preserved during lateral lymph node dissection for rectal adenocarcinoma. Our patient had an uneventful post-operative recovery and was discharged on post-operative day seven. Histopathology revealed a pathological complete response. She is currently kept on observation and is on regular follow-up.

This is our published article in the European Journal of Surgical Oncology which comments on the learning curve for lateral pelvic lymph node dissection. Our study indicates that the learning curve is relatively long and it takes around eighty three cases to achieve the learning curve. The major two factors that increase the nodal harvest is increasing experience of the surgeon and minimally invasive approach.
Thank you very much for your kind attention.
